track.title: Money Foundations
track.description: A beginner path for checking goals, liquidity, risk, pressure, and provider status before money moves.

course.title: Money Foundations: Core route
course.description: Practical money foundations for Kenyan beginners before comparing products.

lessons.title: Why financial access is not the same as financial health
lessons.summary: Having an app, account, or product is different from having a stable plan.
lessons.introduction: Financial access means you can reach products, apps, accounts, or providers. Financial health means your money can still handle rent, food, transport, family needs, emergencies, and slow months without panic.
sections: A person can open many accounts and still be financially fragile if every shilling is already committed before payday.
Before comparing products, write the goal, timeline, amount range, and what would make the decision unsafe.
Good financial health usually starts with cash-flow visibility, emergency money, and avoiding pressure rather than chasing a product name.

scenario.title: First-jobber example
scenario.text: A new employee has M-Pesa, a bank account, and several investment apps. They still pause because rent and transport are due before the next salary.

lessons.mistake: Treating account opening as progress even when the money has no clear purpose or emergency buffer.
lessons.action: Write one sentence for the goal and one sentence for what must not be put at risk.
lessons.takeaway: Access is useful, but health comes from purpose, timing, liquidity, and discipline.
lessons.source_note: Source note: This is PesaRoute editorial literacy guidance; verify product-specific rules with official providers.

lessons.title: Emergency fund basics
lessons.summary: Emergency money should be easy to access and separate from risky or locked decisions.
lessons.introduction: An emergency fund is money kept aside for real disruptions: illness, job delay, rent stress, transport problems, school needs, or family shocks. It should be reachable when needed.
sections: The main job of emergency money is access, not excitement. A slightly boring place can be useful if withdrawals are predictable.
Do not mix emergency money with money you can leave for months or years. The timeline is different.
A starter buffer can be small. Even KES 1,000 to KES 5,000 clearly separated teaches the habit.

scenario.title: Matatu and clinic week
scenario.text: A student has KES 3,500 saved. If a clinic visit and transport issue happen in the same week, locked money will not help quickly.

lessons.mistake: Putting emergency money where withdrawal timing, fees, or market prices could delay access.
lessons.action: Choose one emergency range you want to protect before considering any product.
lessons.takeaway: Emergency money is not about maximum return; it is about staying stable when life interrupts.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify withdrawal rules with any provider before relying on money for emergencies.

lessons.title: Risk, return, and liquidity
lessons.summary: Compare what can go right, what can go wrong, and how quickly you can exit.
lessons.introduction: Most money decisions balance three ideas: possible return, risk, and liquidity. A product can sound attractive but still be wrong if the money may be needed soon.
sections: Return is what you may earn, but it can vary and should not be treated as a promise.
Risk includes loss, delay, fraud, fees, inflation, currency movement, or being unable to exit.
Liquidity is how quickly and predictably you can access money without a large cost.

scenario.title: KES 20,000 comparison
scenario.text: A first-jobber compares an MMF, a T-bill, and shares. The emergency need points toward liquidity, while long-term money can tolerate more waiting.

lessons.mistake: Looking only at possible return and ignoring whether the money can be accessed when needed.
lessons.action: For any option, write one return point, one risk point, and one liquidity point.
lessons.takeaway: A calm comparison includes upside, downside, and exit before money moves.
lessons.source_note: Source note: CMA investor education materials explain market products and investor protection concepts.

lessons.title: How to compare options before sending money
lessons.summary: Use a simple comparison before sending money to any route.
lessons.introduction: Comparison protects you from pressure. Before sending money, compare goal fit, provider status, fees, liquidity, documents, and what can go wrong.
sections: Start with the goal. Emergency, school fees, business float, and long-term learning do not need the same product.
Check the provider or route using official sources where available: regulator lists, government portals, exchange information, or provider documents.
Write the exit rule in plain language. If you cannot explain how money comes back, pause.

scenario.title: WhatsApp offer pause
scenario.text: A chama member receives an offer in a group chat. Instead of sending a deposit, they ask who regulates it, what fees apply, and how withdrawals work.

lessons.mistake: Comparing only one number, such as yield or expected profit, instead of the whole route.
lessons.action: Create a four-row comparison: goal fit, provider check, fees/tax, exit timing.
lessons.takeaway: A product is not ready for your money until the route is clear.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify official route details with CMA, CBK, SASRA, NSE/CDSC, provider documents, or a licensed professional.

lessons.title: Guaranteed returns are a red flag
lessons.summary: Treat promised high payouts, urgency, and secrecy as warning signs.
lessons.introduction: A pitch that promises high payouts while hiding risk is not asking you to learn. It is asking you to trust pressure. Serious products explain risks, fees, provider status, and exit rules.
sections: Watch for urgent deadlines, referral rewards, screenshots, and claims that questions show you lack ambition.
Legitimate investing still has risk, delay, costs, paperwork, and changing outcomes.
If the person cannot explain who regulates the product and how withdrawals work, do not send money.

scenario.title: Screenshot profit pitch
scenario.text: A friend forwards a screenshot and says the window closes tonight. The safer response is to ask for public registration details and refuse pressure.

lessons.mistake: Sending a deposit because the story sounds urgent or because other people appear to have joined.
lessons.action: Paste the pitch into the scam checker and list three questions before replying.
lessons.takeaway: Pressure is information. If a pitch rushes you, slow down.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify investment firms and market players using official public sources such as CMA where relevant.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which comparison is most complete before money moves?
quiz_questions.explanation: A learning-first comparison includes what can go right, what can go wrong, and how quickly money can be accessed.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should you do if you cannot explain how money comes back?
quiz_questions.explanation: Exit timing and withdrawal rules matter before committing money.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which sign should make you slow down?
quiz_questions.explanation: Urgency can be a red flag when it replaces clear information.

flashcards.back: How quickly and predictably money can be accessed without a large cost.
flashcards.example: Emergency money needs higher liquidity than long-term learning money.

flashcards.back: The chance of loss, delay, fraud, cost, or an outcome worse than expected.
flashcards.example: A locked product can be risky for school-fee money.

flashcards.back: What money may earn, after considering fees and taxes where applicable.
flashcards.example: A quoted yield is not the same as money already received.

flashcards.back: Goal, timeline, amount range, and exit need.
flashcards.example: Rent money and long-term investing money should not be treated the same.

flashcards.back: It shows the seller may want speed instead of clear questions.
flashcards.example: If a pitch punishes you for asking about regulation, pause.

resources.title: First salary money checklist
resources.body: A starter checklist for separating needs, emergency money, family support, debt, and learning money before choosing products.
items: Set aside rent, food, transport, and debt first.
Keep family support clear and written.
Use learning money only after essentials are protected.

resources.title: Scam red flags cheat sheet
resources.body: A quick red-flag reference for pressure, secrecy, referral rewards, unclear withdrawals, missing regulator details, and requests for PINs or passwords.
items: No M-Pesa PINs, bank passwords, OTPs, or remote-control apps.
Ask what can go wrong and how withdrawals work.
Do not send money under urgency.

glossary.definition: How quickly and predictably you can access money without a large cost.
glossary.example: An emergency fund needs liquidity because rent or hospital money may be needed quickly.

glossary.definition: The chance that an outcome is worse than expected, including loss, delay, cost, fraud, or inability to exit.
glossary.example: Buying shares with rent money creates risk even if the company is well known.

glossary.definition: What money may earn after considering fees, taxes, timing, and changing market conditions.
glossary.example: An MMF quoted yield can change, so it should not be treated as money already received.

glossary.definition: Spreading exposure so one company, asset, sector, or route does not decide the whole outcome.
glossary.example: A person may avoid putting all long-term money into one share or one land deal.

glossary.definition: A licensed firm or person responsible for managing pooled investment money under a fund mandate.
glossary.example: A Kenyan unit trust or MMF should identify the fund manager in its documents.

glossary.definition: An institution that holds or safeguards assets for a fund or investor according to the product structure.
glossary.example: A unit trust document may name a custodian separate from the fund manager.

glossary.definition: A party that helps protect investor interests in a collective investment scheme structure.
glossary.example: MMF documents may name a trustee in addition to the fund manager and custodian.

glossary.definition: A pooled investment scheme where investors buy units and share exposure to the fund assets.
glossary.example: Many Kenyan MMFs are offered as unit trust funds.

glossary.definition: A Money Market Fund that pools investor money mainly into short-term instruments.
glossary.example: A first-jobber may compare MMFs for short-term savings, but should check fees and withdrawal timing.

glossary.definition: A short-term government security usually sold at a discount and repaid at face value at maturity.
glossary.example: A 91-day T-bill may not suit money needed before the maturity date.

glossary.definition: A longer-term government security that usually pays coupon interest during its life and principal at maturity.
glossary.example: A bond investor should understand coupon dates, price risk, and maturity.

glossary.definition: Interest paid by a bond according to its terms.
glossary.example: A fixed-rate Treasury bond may pay coupons on scheduled dates.

glossary.definition: A measure of return that can depend on price, time, fees, tax, and market conditions.
glossary.example: A T-bill yield and an MMF quoted yield are not the same kind of number.

glossary.definition: The date when a security is due to repay principal according to its terms.
glossary.example: Money needed next month may not fit a product maturing in 182 days.

glossary.definition: The Central Bank of Kenya portal used for government securities access and account services.
glossary.example: A user should verify current T-bill instructions on CBK or DhowCSD before applying.

glossary.definition: An electronic account used to hold securities such as listed shares in the central depository system.
glossary.example: A beginner buying NSE shares needs to understand the CDS account route.

glossary.definition: The Central Depository & Settlement Corporation that maintains the central depository system for Kenyan securities.
glossary.example: CDSC records help support electronic custody and settlement of shares.

glossary.definition: The Nairobi Securities Exchange, where listed securities are traded through approved market participants.
glossary.example: A beginner should verify a trading participant before opening a share-trading route.

glossary.definition: Member ownership contribution that may have restrictions and may not behave like ordinary savings.
glossary.example: A SACCO may require share capital before loan eligibility, but exit rules matter.

glossary.definition: Member savings or contributions held under SACCO rules, often linked to loan eligibility and withdrawal terms.
glossary.example: A member should ask how long deposit withdrawal takes before joining.

glossary.definition: A distribution from an entity to members or shareholders, subject to rules and performance.
glossary.example: A SACCO dividend history should not be treated as a promise for next year.

glossary.definition: A SACCO return related to interest paid by members, depending on the SACCO's rules and performance.
glossary.example: Members should ask how rebates are calculated and approved.

glossary.definition: A person who agrees to support another member's loan obligation if the borrower fails to meet terms.
glossary.example: Do not guarantee a loan you cannot afford to support.

glossary.definition: A group that pools contributions for savings, lending, investment, or mutual support.
glossary.example: A chama needs minutes, voting rules, and a clear investment policy before buying assets.

glossary.definition: A real estate investment trust that pools money into real estate assets under a regulated structure.
glossary.example: A beginner should read the offer documents and market route before treating a REIT like direct land.

glossary.definition: An exchange-traded fund that offers exposure to a basket of assets through a market-traded instrument.
glossary.example: A global ETF route adds platform, currency, tax, and custody questions.

glossary.definition: The risk that exchange-rate movement changes your result when money crosses currencies.
glossary.example: A USD investment can gain in dollars while the KES conversion still changes the outcome.

glossary.definition: Tax deducted at source from certain payments according to applicable tax rules.
glossary.example: A user should check KRA or a tax professional for current tax treatment of interest or dividends.

glossary.definition: The process of checking documents, people, sources, costs, and risks before committing money.
glossary.example: Land due diligence may include search, survey, seller identity, and independent legal review.

glossary.definition: A public authority that supervises a sector or type of financial activity.
glossary.example: CMA, CBK, SASRA, IRA, and RBA supervise different parts of Kenya's financial system.

glossary.definition: A scheme that uses deception, pressure, or false claims to make people send money.
glossary.example: A pitch that hides provider details and rushes deposits should be checked before any payment.
